title: How the suprachiasmatic nucleus disrupts circadian rhythms after trauma
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The clock that forgot how to keep time 

 I used to think my sleep problems were just bad habits. Late night scrolling. Too much coffee. Not enough discipline....
metaDescription:
The clock that forgot how to keep time 

 I used to think my sleep problems were just bad habits. Late night scrolling. Too much coffee. Not enough discip...
htmlContent:
The clock that forgot how to keep time

I used to think my sleep problems were just bad habits. Late night scrolling. Too much coffee. Not enough discipline. But here's the thing. Trauma doesn't just mess with your memories or your relationships. It rewires the most basic biological rhythms your body runs on. And I mean the really basic ones. The ones you never think about until they break.

There's a tiny cluster of about 20,000 neurons deep in your brain called the suprachiasmatic nucleus. The SCN for short. It sits in the hypothalamus, right above where your optic nerves cross. And its job is simple. It's your master clock. The conductor of your entire circadian orchestra. Every cell in your body listens to it. Your heart. Your liver. Your immune system. Your adrenal glands. They all wait for the SCN to tell them what time it is.

But after trauma, something shifts. The SCN doesn't stop working. It just starts working differently. It becomes hypersensitive to threat cues. It loses its steady rhythm. And when the master clock falters, everything downstream falters with it. Your sleep. Your digestion. Your hormone regulation. Your ability to feel safe at night. All of it.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, has written extensively about how trauma freezes the body in a state of incomplete defensive responses. Your nervous system stays locked in a loop, scanning for danger even when danger has passed. And the SCN, being the sensitive timekeeper it is, picks up on this. It adjusts. It shifts your sleep-wake cycle toward hypervigilance. It starts treating dusk as a threat rather than a signal for rest. Wild, right?

Why your body thinks nighttime is dangerous

Think about what happens when you experience something terrifying. Your sympathetic nervous system kicks in. Your heart races. Your pupils dilate. Your cortisol spikes. Your body releases glucose for quick energy. You're ready to fight or run. That's normal. That's adaptive. The problem is when your brain never gets the signal that the threat is over.

For many trauma survivors, nighttime becomes a trigger. Darkness meant danger once. Or vulnerability. Or the moment when the bad thing happened. Your SCN doesn't reason about this. It doesn't think, "Well, I'm safe now, it's 2024 and I'm in my own bed." No. It responds to patterns. And if your body has learned that nighttime equals threat, the SCN will shift your circadian rhythm accordingly.

You might find yourself wide awake at 2 AM. Or exhausted by 6 PM but unable to fall asleep. Or waking up multiple times a night with your heart pounding. These aren't just random sleep problems. They are your master clock trying to protect you. It's doing its job. The job just happens to be calibrated for a world that no longer exists.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has talked about how trauma survivors often have disrupted cortisol rhythms. Normally, cortisol peaks in the morning to help you wake up and drops at night to let you sleep. But after trauma, that rhythm flattens. Cortisol stays elevated when it should be low. Melatonin production gets suppressed. Your body doesn't get the chemical signals it needs to transition into rest. A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). It explains this connection between trauma and biological disruption in ways that made me feel less crazy about what was happening in my own body.

Stay with me here. The SCN doesn't just control sleep. It controls the timing of almost every physiological process. Body temperature. Blood pressure. Immune function. Digestion. When your SCN gets thrown off by trauma, everything gets thrown off. You might notice you get hungry at weird times. Or your body temperature fluctuates oddly. Or you get sick more often. It's all connected. The master clock touches everything.

Light, darkness, and the broken dial

Here's how the SCN normally works. Light hits your eyes. Special cells in your retina, called photosensitive retinal ganglion cells, send signals directly to the SCN. The SCN then tells your pineal gland to stop producing melatonin. You wake up. Darkness comes. The SCN tells the pineal gland to start producing melatonin. You get sleepy. It's a beautiful, elegant system. It's been evolving for hundreds of millions of years.

But trauma changes how your brain processes sensory information. Your threat detection system becomes oversensitive. Your amygdala, the brain's alarm center, starts overriding the SCN's signals. Even when it's dark and your SCN is saying "time to sleep," your amygdala is saying "danger, stay alert." And the amygdala often wins. Because survival trumps sleep every time.

I've had nights where I was exhausted beyond words. My body ached. My eyes burned. I knew I needed sleep. But the moment I closed my eyes, my heart would start racing. My mind would scan for threats. My body would tense up. That's the amygdala and the SCN in conflict. One wants rest. The other wants vigilance. And they're both trying to keep you alive.

There's also something called the social zeitgeber theory, which was developed by researchers like Timothy Monk and others. It suggests that our social rhythms help keep our biological clocks on track. Regular meal times. Regular work schedules. Regular social interactions. These are cues that help the SCN stay synchronized. But trauma often disrupts these social rhythms too. You might isolate. You might lose your job. You might stop eating regular meals. And without those social time cues, your SCN has even less information to work with.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She's a clinician who studied under Stephen Porges, and her work focuses on how to gently retrain your nervous system toward safety. It's not about forcing yourself to sleep. It's about creating conditions where your SCN can gradually recalibrate.

What actually helps the master clock reset

I'm not going to tell you to just try harder to sleep. That's insulting. And it doesn't work. If willpower could fix trauma-related circadian disruption, none of us would be reading this. So let's talk about what actually has some evidence behind it.

Morning light exposure is one of the most powerful tools. Your SCN needs a strong light signal in the morning to set its rhythm. Not just any light. Bright light. Ideally sunlight. But if you can't get outside, a bright light therapy lamp can help. The key is timing. Within the first hour of waking. For at least 20 to 30 minutes. This tells your SCN that it's morning, and it starts coordinating all your biological processes accordingly.

Temperature also matters. Your body temperature naturally drops at night to sleep. Trauma can disrupt this. Taking a warm bath or shower about 90 minutes before bed can help. The warm water raises your body temperature, and then the rapid cooling afterward mimics the natural temperature drop that signals sleep. It's a hack. But it works for some people.

Magnesium is another piece of the puzzle. Trauma depletes magnesium. Stress uses it up. And magnesium is essential for the nervous system to relax. One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). The glycinate form is specifically helpful because glycine itself has calming effects on the brain. It's not a sedative. It's more like giving your nervous system the raw materials it needs to settle down.

I also want to mention something about inherited trauma patterns. If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link) by Mark Wolynn. He explores how trauma can be passed down through generations, affecting things like your stress response and your biological rhythms. It's not about blaming your parents. It's about understanding why your nervous system might be calibrated the way it is.

Living with a clock that tells a different time

Honestly, I don't think the goal is to fix your circadian rhythm completely. Not right away. Not perfectly. The goal is to start building a relationship with your body's timekeeping system that isn't based on fighting it. You can't bully your SCN into submission. It's not a bad employee that needs to be managed. It's a sensitive organ that has been doing its best to protect you.

Some days your sleep will be terrible. Some nights you'll wake up at 3 AM and not be able to go back to sleep. That's not a failure. That's your body still learning that it's safe. Your SCN is slowly, slowly recalibrating. It takes time. It takes consistency. It takes compassion for the part of you that still thinks nighttime is dangerous.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your chest tighten? Did your shoulders relax a little? That's your nervous system responding to the possibility of safety. That's your SCN getting a tiny signal that maybe, just maybe, it can start to trust the darkness again.

I can't promise you perfect sleep. I can't promise you a rhythm that runs like clockwork. But I can tell you that your SCN is not broken. It's not defective. It's just been doing a job that no longer needs doing. And with time, with patience, with the right conditions, it can learn a new rhythm. One that lets you rest. One that lets you heal. One that lets the darkness be just darkness again.

Healing Journey Resources

  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link) - Mark Wolynn's exploration of inherited trauma patterns
  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's foundational book on trauma and the body
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to nervous system regulation
  • Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link) - A supplement that supports nervous system calm
